data.title: GSI Technology: Is The APU A Game Changer Or Just Noise In The Market?
data.description:
GSI Technology (NASDAQ:GSIT) issued a major update to its associative processing unit [APU] technology in development on October 20, 2025, sending the share price up 260%+ to over $18/share before settling down to roughly $13/share at the end of the trading day on October 20, 2025, creating significant interest amongst shareholders. Shares have since declined by nearly -40% from the peak share price after GSIT announced on the following day a $50mm share and pre-funded warrant registration, pricing the shares and warrants at $10/share and $9.99/warrant. Given the significant run-up in the share price, I am recommending GSIT shares with a Sell rating, pricing shares at the midcycle premium of $4.33/share at 4.05x eFY27 price/sales. It should be noted that my rating downgrade is purely tied to valuation and not GSIT's current operational state.

GSIT will announce q2’26 earnings October 30, 2025.

You can review my previous coverage of GSI Technology here:

GSI Technology Is Pushing For A Recovery In A Challenging Market

GSI Technology Operational Update

There will be key factors that I will be looking for in eq2’26 earnings:

  • APU CIM applications and potential adoption plan.
  • Go-to-market strategy.
  • Capacity updates to service the addressable market.
  • Technology partnerships for implementation.

The market-moving announcement on October 20, 2025, was the published results of a research paper through Cornell University, finding that GSIT’s APU compute-in-memory architecture can “match GPU-level performance for large-scale AI applications” with 98% lower energy consumption. The press release indicates that the APU performance is comparable to the Nvidia (NVDA) RTX A6000, a GPU designed for desktop workstations.

Taking into consideration the verbiage of the press release and the compared technology, I believe investors should be aware of the full context of the performance comparison. The press release suggested that the APU CIM technology “has the potential to disrupt the $100b AI inference market.” Investors should consider that the study compared GSIT’s APU CIM to Nvidia’s workstation GPU rather than its data center GPUs like the H100, GB200 or GB300, a potentially narrower growth market relative to the AI data center market. For Nvidia, the Professional Visualization market segment was only a $2.1b business in the last twelve months, accounting for only 1.28% of net revenue.

The reason I bring this up isn’t hinting that GSIT may take over market share from Nvidia but rather, raising the question as to why the share prices of major edge compute chipmakers like Intel (INTC), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), or Nvidia didn’t react to the breaking news. Granted, the market for GSIT’s APU may be beyond the workstation, targeting markets like robotics, drones, IoT devices, and aerospace & defense applications that require high-capacity computing while infrastructure resources may be limited. This can particularly be an appealing technology for drones and aerospace & defense technologies, particularly as more equipment becomes electrified.

Drones are quickly becoming a larger interest for national defense and military operations as the battlefield shifts more towards advanced technologies. The Department of War and NASA are currently collaborating on developing autonomous, long-distance cargo transportation drones that can fly beyond visual line of sight.

In addition to this, drone and counter-drone technology is being invested as part of the national defense effort as foreign actors rely more heavily on the technology. Some of the largest challenges with drone countermeasures have been the size and capabilities of a drone strike. The ultimate question that could drive GSIT’s business would be whether AI-enabled autonomous drones can effectively counter a drone strike. If this is the case, GSIT may be well positioned to realize a niche positioning in a growing market. According to its most recent investor slide deck, the drone & satellite AI chips & enterprise edge data centers market can grow from $6.8b in 2025 to $16.4b by 2030.

The ultimate question that investors must consider is whether GSIT can deliver. The firm had previously voiced supply chain challenges in q1’26 that may have impacted the firm’s ability to fully deliver on customer orders. Though having more demand for one’s technology than supply may not be the worst position to be in, not being able to service the volumes in demand isn’t much better, especially when considering the scale of GSIT and the large market share potential the firm is reaching for. Overall, I believe GSIT will need to invest heavily in the supply chain and deepen its partnerships with chipmakers like Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) in order to impactfully bring its APU to market. Given the significant potential for disrupting the current technology providers, GSIT will need to go all-in on its APU CIM in order to achieve any meaningful technological disruption as management voiced in the press release.

In my opinion, one alternative could be for the technology to be acquired by a major chip designer like Intel, which recently raised roughly $16b through investments by the US Government, Nvidia, and SoftBank Group. Perhaps Intel has the firepower to provide GSIT an offer it cannot refuse to kick-start its Client Compute business after peaking in FY21.

GSI Technology Financial Position

Looking ahead to eq2’26, I’m maintaining my previous financial forecast with revenue coming in at $6.3mm with an adjusted EPS of -$0.07/share. Despite the recent proof of function report by Cornell for GSIT APUs, the market will determine the technology’s feasibility for its functionality and price point.

One factor that may raise concerns is the timing of the shelf offering announced on October 21, 2025, one day following the Cornell Research press release. According to the release, GSIT will raise roughly $50mm through a 1.5mm common stock offering and the sale of pre-funded warrants to purchase nearly 3.5mm common shares, pricing shares at $10/share. The capital raised through the issuances will go towards covering corporate expenses as well as the development of the GSIT APU product line.

Risks Related to GSI Technology

Bull Case

The viability of GSIT APUs is becoming increasingly apparent, potentially driving significant growth in niche markets that may require high-performance compute capacity with low power utilization.

Bear Case

GSIT must prove that it can bring its advanced APU product to market by overcoming the supply chain challenges faced in q1’26 while expanding its go-to-market strategy.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GSIT shares currently trade at 13.23x price/sales, a significant premium over their historical trading average. Ultimately, I believe shares may experience some pullback given the significant run-up following the APU research update. In my view, the press release moved GSIT into a growth tech premium stock, requiring the company to deliver on the necessary growth and product adoption in order for operations to normalize the price premium, or face significant share price decline.

Given the historical risks associated with GSIT’s supply chain and the niche markets its APUs are set to compete in, I am recommending GSIT shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $4.33/share at 4.05x eFY27 price/sales, roughly the midcycle price premium.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Nebius Pullback: The Smart Money Entry Point
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) sits at the turning point of a trillion-dollar shift in global cloud infrastructure. With hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) hitting power and compliance limits, Nebius' sovereign-first foundation, along with its $17.4 billion historical deal with Microsoft, is quietly reshaping compute distribution in the era of AI. Its AI Cloud 3.0 marks a shift from renting GPUs to building a full technology stack, aiming to capture higher-margin, long-term software revenue.

Institutional capital started cycling back since dark-pool inflows and options data confirm new confidence. After a 30% pullback from its $141 high last month, Nebius presents a balanced high-risk, high-reward opportunity for investors ahead of its next potential re-rating cycle. Nebius remains a strong buy as a new emerging sovereign foundation of global AI infrastructure.

The End of Centralization: How Neoclouds Are Rewriting the AI Infrastructure Map

For over a decade, the hyperscaler formula, designed for global scale, dominated cloud computing. But the emerging world of generative AI has shattered that formula. Such compute- and power-hungry models as GPT-5 and Gemini no longer allow for older, centralized architectures to scale. Outrageous power expenses, rigid data-residency regulations, and latency constraints have made it economically imprudent to scale classic hyperscale data centers across international borders.

A new wave of “neoclouds” has emerged. Local, efficient providers that offer secure data control, fast performance, and the same reliability as major hyperscalers. Last year, the neocloud market revenue grew 54% and will compound at over 45% annually from 2024 to 2030, Mordor Intelligence estimates.

The Option Market Spoke: Institutional Buying Following the Retract

The 30% correction from Nebius’ September highs raised questions about whether its fundamentals still supported the earlier rally. Options and dark-pool data, however, point to signs of renewed accumulation.

Institutional investors seem to be setting up for the upside of Nebius, where intense call-option buying hints at fresh confidence. Most active range falls within the $90 and $110 strikes, where the biggest funds are accommodated at $11.7 million open interest at the latter and another $8.5 million at the earlier. The $50 strike activity reflects deep-in-the-money synthetic long positions, a strategy that mirrors stock ownership while requiring less capital. Total open call contracts are higher above 420,000, while the bear puts contracted at 253,000, an indication those are unmistakably institutional confidence and not speculation.

Whalestream reports that approximately $100 million of net inflows were concentrated within the $103.90 area, indicating fresh institutional interest. This was confirmed by Fintel data (extracted from Nasdaq, FINRA, and Capital IQ), which records the off-exchange short-volume ratio near 19%, and reinforces the thesis of significant Nebius buying through dark pools by the institutions. In practice, this supports the view that much of the Nebius buying is happening quietly through dark pools, as aligned with the observed build-up of call options.

In short, as the retail frenzy faded from the post-summer bubble pop, smart money appears to be entering back in the same place where the risk-adjusted gain is highest. This isn’t speculative money; it’s backed by improving fundamentals.

Nebius' Strategy Alignment: From Local Expert to Microsoft's Sovereign Back Booster

When Microsoft signed its five-year $17.4 billion agreement with Nebius, up to $19.4 billion with expansion, it did more than compute outsourcing. It remapped its supply chain. Nebius is going to deliver AI infrastructure for Microsoft's internal large-language-model build as well as its future consumer AI assistant, basically becoming part of Microsoft's compute fabric extension.

This agreement provides Nebius with twin strategic benefits, with multi-year cash visibility and credibility baked into the hyperscale landscape. With most small players bogged down in grid access and permitting, Nebius' sites in Finland, Serbia, and Israel addressed AI infrastructure’s challenges of energy reliability and latency. With locations in renewable-friendly jurisdictions and proprietary energy-management technology, Nebius is already able to price long-term GPU rents lower than peers like CoreWeave (CRWV).

Lastly, the Nebius AI Cloud 3.0 “Aether” launch represents a strategic turn from capacity leasing to orchestration. With the enterprise security standard, granular IAM, native observability, and policy-based governance, regulated enterprises can deploy and manage scale production AI workloads with Aether. Therefore, Nebius' pivot to a full-stack enterprise platform, evident in its Aether 3.0 launch, positions it as the sovereign substitute to AWS Bedrock or Azure OpenAI Service.

The Sector Characterized with Constraints rather than Competition

The least-recognized aspect of the neocloud market is that its expansion stems from constraints rather than competition. There's significantly more demand for regionalized, GPU-dense infrastructure than supply in the marketplace. Supply-chain unreliability in advanced-node chips, -4.1% effect on CAGR per Mordor Intelligence, is still the largest bottleneck, followed by cross-border localizations (-3.2%) and increased green-datacenter capex (-2.3%).

These supply constraints give early movers like Nebius a real advantage in securing power, hardware, and regulatory approvals. Nebius, through advance allocation of capacity within low-cost, energy-rich jurisdictions like Finland and Serbia, has literally secured the scarce inputs that others would find hard to procure.

Just as important, Nebius’ sovereign-compliant design positions it well to benefit from coming regulatory changes. More than 45 nations are projected to implement sovereignty-based data restrictions by late 2025 or early 2026, representing an estimated $8.2 billion in addressable opportunity by 2030. Unlike hyperscalers that must retrofit compliance across sprawling global systems, Nebius was purpose-built for sovereignty from day one.

Risk Assessment: Pains of Scale Under the Growth Narrative

Though execution is robust, Nebius is subject to the usual scaling ills of an early AI-infrastructure pioneer. GPU supply is tight, with continued demand for Nvidia's H100 and upcoming Blackwell processors leading availability by a wide margin, limiting near-term capacity increases. In response, Nebius aims to bring 220 megawatts of powered-up capacity on-line through 2025 year-end, half already on-line, with 1 GW capacity through 2026 end-date across new data center construction in Finland, New Jersey, Britain, and Israel. Its fast-paced buildout underlies its elevated annualized run-rate revenue forecast of $900 million to $1.1 billion and lays the groundwork for a mid-term multi-billion-dollar revenue possibility.

While Nebius still relies on large strategic contracts, its growing partnerships with companies like Cloudflare, Shopify, and Prosus are helping diversify revenue sources. Increasing green data center capex and regulatory complexity across Europe and the Middle East may erode margins, but Nebius’ renewable-powered sites and sovereign-compliant design insulate much of that risk. Management expects Nebius to come out with positive adjusted EBITDA in 2026, a sign of disciplined expansion in a market experiencing temporary, not structural, issues.

Takeaway

Nebius is turning out to be a major beneficiary of the trillion-dollar neocloud revolution. Its $17.4 billion Microsoft partnership, sovereign-first strategy, and rapid Aether 3.0 expansion provide multi-year visibility. With institutional investors returning after a 30% correction, Nebius offers meaningful upside as a foundational player in the next phase of global AI expansion.

data.title: AMETEK: Shares Are Too Expensive To Justify An Upgrade
data.description:
Emotion can be a big hindrance when it comes to making wise investment decisions. When you find yourself fascinated by a company because of its business model and its track record, it's hard not to like it. But this is entirely separate from it making a good investment opportunity. A great example of this can be seen by looking at AMETEK (NYSE:AME), a firm that sells analytical, test, and measurement instruments, as well as thermal management systems, automation solutions, specialty metals, and more. Over the last couple of years now, this intriguing company has achieved attractive top-line and bottom-line growth. And thanks to its latest acquisition, that trend is almost certain to continue. Relative to other similar firms, shares are not all that pricey. But on an absolute basis, it's more expensive than I would like it to be.

Because of its valuation, I have historically been more neutral on the firm. Whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I am taking the position that, over the long run, shares should perform more or less in line with the broader market as defined by the S&P 500. And even though the stock has fallen far short of that since I last reaffirmed it as a "Hold" in January 2023, with the stock up 34% while the S&P 500 is up 66.3%, the picture looks different when we look at it using a longer term horizon. In fact, since I originally rated it a "Hold" in December 2021, shares are up 39.5%. That is below the 48.2% increase that the broader market experienced. But given the amount of time that we are talking about, I would say that this is not horribly off by any means.

Today, I am reaffirming my "Hold" rating on the stock. Shares are too expensive for my liking, even with the growth that management has achieved. But my opinion can change as new data comes in. Before the market opens on Oct. 30, management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. They expect revenue, profits, and adjusted profits to all improve on a year-over-year basis. But even with that factored in, I am not ready to change my assessment.

A great business

To me, AMETEK can only be described as a great business. This firm has established a pretty good track record over the last couple of years now. Back in 2023, the company generated revenue of $6.60 billion. But last year, that figure grew to $6.94 billion. That rise of 5.2% was caused by a 7% benefit that the company saw thanks to acquisitions. This was offset to some extent by a 2% drop in organic revenue. When it comes to the individual operating segments of the business, we have two separate units to consider. The first and largest of these is its Electronic Instruments (or EIG) segment, which sells advanced instruments for the process, power, industrial, and aerospace markets. The smaller one is the Electromechanical (or EMG) segment, which focuses more on supplying precision motion control solutions, certain medical components and devices, thermal management systems, specialty metals, and more.

From 2023 to 2024, its EIG segment reported a small increase in revenue from $4.62 billion to $4.66 billion. Acquisitions did all the heavy lifting here, while organic revenue dropped by 1% because of weaker demand. The real growth driver during this time was its EMG segment, which reported a 15.6% jump in revenue from $1.97 billion to $2.28 billion. But once again, the business dealt with weak organic revenue as demand for its engineered solutions operations declined and as customer inventories became more normalized following strong demand after the COVID-19 pandemic. The big driver here ended up being its acquisitions, which added 20% to the top line.

As with most businesses, AMETEK saw profitability jump as revenue increased. Net income grew from $1.31 billion to $1.38 billion. Adjusted net profits increased from $1.48 billion to $1.59 billion. As the first chart in this article illustrates, all cash flow metrics for the company improved as well. This bodes well for shareholders, and this year the trend has continued.

During the first half of 2025, revenue for the enterprise came in at $3.51 billion. That's 1.1% above the $3.47 billion the company reported the previous year. Not surprisingly, EMG contributed a lot to this, with revenue growing 4% from $1.16 billion to $1.21 billion thanks to organic sales expanding. At the same time, there was a small drop in revenue associated with EIG, with a decline from $2.31 billion to $2.30 billion. Regardless, profitability for the business continued to get better. Net income, adjusted net income, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA all improved year over year. The only weak spot for the company ended up being its operating cash flow, which dropped from $791.7 million to $776.6 million.

Expect continued growth

Management has been a team on a mission. The company has worked hard to continue growing the company, with a lot of that growth coming from acquisitions. In fact, 75% of the $12 billion that the company has deployed since 2016 has been dedicated towards strategic purchases. That compares to just 14% dedicated to dividends and 11% for share buybacks. Honestly, I love seeing this. I much prefer investments in growth, especially during times when shares are pricey.

The latest such purchase that the company made was of FARO Technologies, a technology solutions provider that offers customers 3D measurement and imaging solutions. This purchase was announced in May of this year at a significant premium to the company’s share price. That premium of 40% valued FARO Technologies at $920 million on an enterprise value basis. That acquisition was completed in late July and brought in about $342.4 million in annual revenue. Based on the data provided for last year, EBITDA should be around $22.3 million, while adjusted operating cash flow should amount to $27.4 million.

Thanks in part to this purchase, management expects revenue this year to grow at a mid-single-digit rate. They also expect adjusted earnings per share of between $7.06 and $7.20. Because of the terms of the purchase of FARO Technologies, the impact on the company's bottom line from a net profit and adjusted operating cash flow perspective should be more or less nil. So I don't think any adjustments are needed there. This would mean adjusted net profits of around $1.65 billion for this year, which would be up from the $1.59 billion the company reported for 2024. We should also be anticipating adjusted operating cash flow of about $1.90 billion. And if we add on another half of a year for FARO Technologies, we would be looking at EBITDA of somewhere around $2.30 billion.

Using these estimates and also using pro forma results for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of AMETEK are currently valued. These are multiples that are, quite frankly, quite lofty in my opinion. But it is important to keep in mind relative valuation as well. In this respect, the stock isn't so bad. In the table below, you can see AMETEK stacked up against five other similar firms. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, it's near the cheaper end of the spectrum. But when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it's the second most expensive of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
AMETEK 25.5 22.2 20.1
Emerson Electric Co. (EMR) 27.9 23.3 17.5
Rockwell Automation (ROK) 40.9 26.0 24.7
Hubbell International (HUBB) 28.1 24.3 18.0
Nextracker (NXT) 24.6 22.0 17.8
Acuity Inc. (AYI) 29.1 19.2 15.6

Lean into earnings

At the start of this article, I notified you that management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year before the market opens on Oct. 30. Presently, analysts expect revenue to come in at around $1.81 billion. That would be up from the $1.71 billion reported the previous year, and some of that will certainly come from its latest purchase. Earnings per share, meanwhile, are forecasted to grow from $1.47 to $1.53, which would take net profits up from $340.2 million to $354.2 million. The table below shows a similar increase when it comes to adjusted earnings per share and overall adjusted net profits. That table also illustrates what the cash flows for the company were in the third quarter of last year. If profitability does truly increase, it would be very likely that adjusted net profits will rise as well.

Takeaway

At its core, AMETEK is a great, growth-oriented business. I love this, and I think that the markets that it operates in are fascinating. But for me, shares are nowhere near cheap enough to be optimistic. The stock is rather pricey, and even factoring in future growth prospects for the rest of this year did not make it cheap enough to justify an upgrade. In light of this, I have decided to keep the firm rated a "Hold" for now.

data.title: Atlantic Union Bankshares: Chugging Along
data.description:
We resume our Q3 regional bank coverage today with the just-reported earnings from Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (NYSE:AUB). This was not the best report, but the company is working to incorporate its Sandy Spring acquisition, which has sent revenue numbers much higher, along with more loans and deposits on hand. The company has been actively selling loans this year to clean up asset quality.

While the stock has not done much of anything of late, we do like the growing dividend and the yield here is still north of 4%. Despite being range bound of late, in our opinion the stock makes a great long-term investment, on the back of a growing dividend and pretty good performance on the key metrics. Overall, we see no signs of credit quality concerns here. Let us discuss.

Atlantic Union Bankshares' headline results and net interest income

Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation's revenue was $348.9 million, which was a 60.6% increase but it missed estimates by a surprising $30 million. Of course, this huge surge stemmed from past acquisitions, and most recently, the pickup of Sandy Spring, completed earlier this year. The revenue number also was impacted by the active selling off of loans the company told us about earlier this year. So, the quarter was noisy overall with ongoing merger activities and expenses.

What about margins? Well, it was not bad news, but it was not good. Pretty much net interest margin was flat from the sequential Q2 (down 1 basis point) at 3.77%. It is a good margin. Cost of funds were down 5 basis points and yields were down a bit more than 5 basis points. The margins on a tax equivalent basis were completely flat at 3.83%, and that level is still ranking high among our universe of coverage. Net interest income was $332.6 million vs. $325.7 million in the sequential Q2 because of them selling off $2 billion in commercial real estate loans.

We should also note that the provision for credit losses improved to $16.2 million, from $105.7 million in Q2. We monitor this metric as provisions do offset earnings power. EPS (adjusted) was $0.82, a slight miss despite the big revenue miss, and helped by lower levels of provisions.

Atlantic Union Bankshares' loans, deposits, and asset quality

At the end of Q3, total loans were $27.3 billion and total deposits were $30.6 billion. Loans were about flat in total with a slight decrease in deposits. Nothing to be concerned with here. How about asset quality?

The allowance for credit losses was up. These allowances were up to $320 million, which was down from $342 million in Q1. A more meaningful metric is the allowance for credit losses as a percentage of all loans and these were 1.17% compared to 1.25% in Q2. This is a notable improvement in our opinion. However net charge-offs jumped to 0.56% of all loans, but this was due to two commercial loans which the company had included in their Q2 loan loss provisions. We suspect the charge-off percent falls back to under 0.10% moving forward.

Return metrics as reported improved. The return on average assets jumped to 0.98% from 0.21% in Q2, a nice move. The return on average equity spiked to 7.51% from 1.67%, and the return on average common tangible equity bounced up to 15.51% from 4.99%. Solid moves, led by an efficiency ratio that improved to 64.28% from 69.42%. So, a noisy quarter, but solid overall.

Final thoughts

The quarter was noisy and merger costs and moves being made are causing some of the key metrics to flop around some. However, asset quality remains strong. And going forward, we still like the bank for its dividend growth. At this level, there is over a 4% yield. We think you can own Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation stock for income/dividend growth long-term.

data.title: Legal & General: Capital Returns Drive Conviction Amid Market Volatility
data.description:
Since we reviewed the first-half results, we have not provided further commentary on Legal & General Group Plc (OTCPK:LGGNF)(OTCPK:LGGNY). Following an 8.87% decline in the share price since that update (Fig. 1), we return today to revisit the equity story and assess recent developments.

Key elements of our overweight include: 1) the planned disposal of non-core assets to streamline the business model, 2) a sharper focus on capturing growth in the institutional retirement market, 3) greater cost efficiency through cross-selling synergies following the integration of the asset-management operations, and 4) disciplined capital deployment, evident in a progressive dividend policy and the continuation of the share-buyback program.

Fig 1

Why are we still positive?

As L&G does not publish nine-month results, we first recap the key takeaways from the first half and then outline why our conviction remains intact.

Starting with the H1 results, L&G beat Wall Street consensus estimates on operating profit, and top management increased its guidance for back-book optimization in profit and the Solvency II ratio. In numbers, L&G EBIT increased by 6% to £859 million. Profit before tax reached £406 million, with core operating earnings per share of 10.94p. EPS was up 9% in H1 compared to last year's results. The Solvency II coverage ratio stood at 217%.

Source: Legal & General Group H1 ppt - Fig 2

Starting with the latest news, the company communicated that Scott Wheway will join the group board in early January 2026. Mr. Wheway is Chair of Scottish Widows and has considerable experience in the financial services and retail businesses. The new chair announcement suggests a greater focus on L&G's retail business, including protection, retirement products, and individual annuities. Related to rumors, we understood that L&G could be considering a Cushon acquisition from NatWest. This business includes workplace pensions and savings businesses. Cushon manages £3.7 billion AuM, an excellent add-on to L&G's >£100 billion workplace pensions business. Strategically, we believe this add-on could help the company to offer a greater suite of products and services to its retail client base. The company has a large workplace business, and in one division, we anticipate a 10% year-on-year growth with an expected 2028 operating earnings target between £500 million and £600 million (Fig. 3).

Over the next twelve months, the company will likely return 17.4% of its current market capitalization. This is a solid investment thesis and one of our EU insurance coverage's most attractive capital return stories. This includes an 8.5% 2026 dividend yield, which was increased by 2% with the interim dividend and £1.2 billion buyback (Fig. 4), equivalent to 8.9% of the current market cap. Although L&G’s shareholders’ equity fell roughly 20% in H1, primarily reflecting write-downs and market effects despite stable bond yields, the company's outlook will improve with the planned disposal of the US Protection business. In our forward view, we estimate equity could trough around £2.3 billion and are not concerned. L&G cash generation remains positive, and the shares trade at about 1.2x total comprehensive equity. On this basis, we see no structural issue.

Lately, the Pension Risk Transfer landscape has been jolted by M&A, with Athora and Brookfield agreeing to buy Pensions Insurance Corporation and Just Group, respectively. Even if competition is higher, there is substantial equity value in the L&G asset portfolio (Fig. 3). The VA segment in L&G is reported in the Institutional Retirement division. In H1 2025, the company reached a profit of £400 million before tax. As reported in Aegon, valuing this business at 9-10x earnings, this business might be equivalent to £8-9 billion. Considering L&G's current market cap of £13 billion, there is upside in a sum-of-the-parts valuation.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

Before going into the details, the CEO reported “L&G had an excellent six months with core operating EPS up 9%, at the top end of our targeted range of 6-9%." For this reason, the company increased its outlook to the high end of its yearly guidance (Fig. 5). We see support for this statement given the CALA disposal, which reduced our previously estimated EPS by 5%. In our estimates, we still forecast a core operating profit of £1.89 billion, and considering a corporate tax rate of 24%, we derive a net income projection of £1.44 billion. The company is currently repurchasing shares. In our last update, L&G had 5.78 billion shares outstanding, and now we see 5.7 billion. With a £1.2 billion buyback over the next year, this will result in an 8.9% share reduction. This will result in a forward outstanding share count of 5.2 billion. So, our adjusted EPS will reach 27.5 cents from a previous forecast of 26.4 cents. Continuing to apply a sector median price/earnings of 10x (Fig. 6), L&G stock price could reach $3.66 (OTCPK:LGGNF) and $18 in ADR (OTCPK:LGGNY). The company is listed in the UK, and our target price is 275p. So, L&G remains a buy.

Fig 5

Fig 6

Risks

L&G downside risks include: a volatile equity market; the company has mostly fee payments based on net asset value (NAV), so a decrease in value means lower fee generation, as well as higher credit risks given L&G's debt portfolio, which is also combined with the current environment in credit spread. Additionally, the company has exposure to private markets; a lower investment result is detrimental to the company's valuation. L&G risks are also execution risks, interest rate development, and rising competition in the UK bulk annuity market, as reported in the analysis. On the balance sheet risks, we report higher regulatory requirements, such as a higher Solvency II ratio, and capital generation not covering capital return, so the company could be forced to reduce its shareholders' remuneration.

Conclusion

We continue to see value in L&G with a higher target price. We are slightly raising our forecasts modestly, as we see limited scope for positive surprises relative to consensus. However, we believe buyback plus dividend yield will be viewed as positive. We think this should power the stock forward over the coming 12 months. Although we are more cautious about relative valuation, management has increased profit guidance. Additionally, the latest market transaction supports the value of L&G assets. Therefore, we remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Falling Mortgage Rates Further Reduce Demand In The Housing Market (Not So Paradoxically)
data.description:
Waiting for even lower mortgage rates comes on top of all the big issues that crush demand in the housing market.

For the fourth week in a row, applications for mortgages to purchase a home fell, despite declining mortgage rates, and hit the lowest level since the week ended July 30, when mortgage rates were nearly 50 basis points higher. The much-ballyhooed idea that lower mortgage rates would unleash waves of demand is running into reality?

Demand by homebuyers began to plunge over three years ago and has been wobbling along very low levels ever since. Compared to the same week in 2019, purchase mortgage applications were down by 35%, according to data from the Mortgage Bankers Association today.

Applications for mortgages to purchase a home are an indicator of home sales in the near future.

But the average weekly mortgage rate for conforming 30-year fixed mortgages declined to 6.37% in the latest week, according to the MBA today.

Since the reporting week ended July 30, mortgage rates have dropped by 46 basis points (from 6.83%); and since the end of May, by 61 basis points (from 6.98%). Yet mortgage applications are now lower than they were then.

Mortgage rates, according to this measure, bottomed out in September 2024 at 6.13%, just before the Fed started cutting interest rates.

Not so paradoxical. The phenomenon that already low demand for mortgages would decline further even though mortgage rates have fallen substantially is not so paradoxical.

Potential homebuyers – those not turned off by the too-high prices – see mortgage rates zigzagging lower, and they listen to the housing prophets promoting the notion that mortgage rates will fall further, and they add 2 and 2 together, and the first thing they do is wait for even lower mortgage rates.

Waiting for even lower mortgage rates comes on top of all the big issues that have crushed demand in the housing market – including way too high prices, though they have started to come down in many markets, and in some markets by a lot, for single-family homes and for condos and co-ops.

Waiting for mortgage rates to decline further is the opposite behavior of the FOMO-driven buying that had set in late 2021 and early 2022, when mortgage rates had started to rise from below 3%, as the Fed was making noises about rate hikes and tapering QE to deal with inflation, which had begun to rage.

Fear of rising mortgage rates at the time, and efforts by homebuyers to “lock in” the still low mortgage rates and buy before prices spiked even further, triggered a huge wave of demand and crazy FOMO-driven buying, which resulted in home prices exploding through the first half of 2022.

So now mortgage rates have fallen, and prices are falling in many markets, and people are waiting for still lower mortgage rates and still lower prices.

But the lower rates increased refis. Applications for mortgages to refinance an existing mortgage ticked up in the latest week, and for the past six weeks have been higher than any time since March 2022. But that spike last month took a lot of pent-up refi demand off the table, it seems.

The September 2024 spike in demand for refi mortgages occurred when the MBA’s measure of the 30-year fixed mortgage rate was even lower than today.

Refi mortgage applications (red in the chart below) rise when mortgage interest rates (blue) fall, and vice versa.

That spike of refi mortgage applications in September was small compared to the boom years of the 3% mortgages that everyone was trying to refinance into. But the current level of demand for refi mortgages is in the middle of the range of three years before the pandemic.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Slips As Weak Earnings And Trade Jitters Test Market Momentum
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​The S&P 500 fell 0.53% on Wednesday to close at 6,699, as disappointing corporate results and renewed geopolitical uncertainty weighed on sentiment. Futures were steady in early Thursday trading, with investors balancing mixed earnings against macro headwinds ahead of Friday’s key U.S. CPI report, which could set the tone for the Federal Reserve’s next policy steps.

Earnings results dominated the session, with Tesla sliding nearly 4% after delivering mixed numbers and cautious guidance, while IBM dropped more than 6% amid weak software revenue. The declines dragged the broader tech sector lower, offsetting gains in cyclical and travel-linked names such as Southwest Airlines and Las Vegas Sands, both of which posted upbeat quarterly results. Moderna, Lam Research, and Molina Healthcare also faced selling pressure, reflecting investor unease about the uneven pace of profit recovery across sectors.

The divergence between cyclical strength and weakness in technology underscores the market’s shifting tone this earnings season. While consumer and travel demand show signs of resilience, margin pressure and cautious forward guidance among major tech firms have kept investors selective, limiting the index’s ability to sustain gains near record levels.

Trade and policy risks weigh on outlook

Adding to the cautious mood were reports that the U.S. may expand export restrictions to China involving key software components, sparking a sell-off across technology shares. The move came just days before President Donald Trump and President Xi Jinping are scheduled to meet, an event markets hope will ease tensions. Trump later reassured investors that talks remain on track, which helped equities trim losses into the close, though traders remain wary of further policy surprises.

The geopolitical backdrop coincides with rising macro uncertainty. Friday’s U.S. inflation report is expected to show headline CPI rising 3.1% year-over-year. A stronger reading could reignite concerns that the Fed’s anticipated December rate cut may be delayed, while a softer print would likely boost risk sentiment.

Technical setup points to a holding phase

Technically, the S&P 500 remains inside a broad ascending channel that has guided its advance since April. Wednesday’s pullback brought prices back to test support at the 20-day EMA near 6,671, which has consistently provided a short-term floor. The 50-day EMA near 6,574 reinforces the next key support zone, with a break below exposing deeper downside toward the 100-day EMA at 6,404. On the upside, resistance remains firm around 6,842 and the upper channel boundary near 6,900–6,920.

S&P 500 price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Momentum indicators suggest the market is entering a consolidation phase. While the index continues to make higher lows, short-term sellers have regained control near the upper resistance levels. Volatility could increase heading into the CPI data, which is likely to determine whether the S&P 500 resumes its advance toward 7,000 or retreats toward its mid-channel support.

Outlook

As discussed in prior sessions, the index’s broader structure remains bullish so long as it holds above the 6,570 floor. The 20- and 50-day moving averages continue to act as critical supports defining the market’s intermediate trend.

For now, the S&P 500 is in a holding pattern, with the next decisive move likely driven by a combination of earnings momentum, inflation data, and central bank policy expectations. A dovish inflation outcome could lift equities toward fresh highs, while persistent macro and trade risks may keep rallies contained in the short term.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Macquarie Asset Strategy Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global markets delivered a strong 3Q25, driven by investors reassessing risks that had characterized the previous quarter. While reaccelerating inflation and weakening labor markets remained key concerns, robust public and private investment in artificial intelligence (AI) continued to buoy sentiment.

July provided a boost in optimism following the prior quarter’s volatility. Resilient US corporate earnings and signs of progress on tariffs helped stabilize markets. Notable policy developments included enactment of the One Big Beautiful Bill in the US and promotion of a pro-growth “involution” campaign (hypercompetitive price wars that have led to diminishing returns and widespread deflationary pressures) by China’s Politburo, both aimed at stimulating domestic industries and consumer activity.

Uncertainty about trade and immigration persisted, as many countries missed a key tariff deadline at the end of July. Labor market ambiguity also clouded economic data. Nevertheless, consumer sentiment rebounded in July from recent lows.

August opened with sharp equity losses as concern over a cooling labor market intensified, a trend that persisted throughout the quarter. Expectations for rate cuts increased following a weaker-than-expected July payroll report, and renewed AI investment plans helped markets recover from initial shocks.

US and European yield curves steepened in July and August amid heightened concerns about budget deficits, debt sustainability, and slowing disinflation. Meanwhile, US credit spreads narrowed. The quarter underscored a growing divergence in quality between corporate and government bonds.

Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, global equity and fixed income markets rose in September. US economic data indicated both a weakening labor market and a resilient consumer. After the Federal Reserve’s 0.25% rate cut in mid-September, expectations for further cuts fluctuated, but investors maintained a bullish outlook, further supported by ongoing AI investment.

Emerging markets outperformed year to date, including in 2Q25, benefiting from a weaker US dollar. Optimism generated by China’s Involution campaign provided additional stimulus.

Oil prices ended the quarter lower, occasionally rallying on sanctions and tariff threats, but ultimately weighed down by rising OPEC+ supply. Gold rallied as the market’s preferred safe-haven asset, offering greater insulation from inflation than US Treasurys.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Asset Strategy Fund generated positive returns but underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI ACWI Index (net).

The Fund’s underperformance was largely driven by the allocation to fixed income securities and stock selection within the equity portfolio. Fixed income, which provides critical correlation and volatility benefits in certain environments, is expected to drag in significant up markets, like the one experienced in 3Q. The Fund’s portfolio holds a meaningful allocation to gold, which was an area of strength and provided a tailwind to returns.

Stock selection proved challenging across most sectors this quarter with impacts from select stocks owned in the Fund and several highflyers that were not owned. This was particularly true within the consumer discretionary sector, where Chinese internet consumer platforms rallied, alongside Tesla (not owned) in the US, while performance among our luxury goods holdings was soft. Within technology, we had reduced our semiconductor exposure on strength, but the group saw continued outperformance amid AI tailwinds. In contrast, the debate continued for software, an area of exposure for the Fund that was challenged during the quarter. Within communication services, the Fund’s telecommunications stocks, normally defensive in nature, broke lower and trailed the benchmark while benchmark heavyweight Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(an underweight) surged.

Among individual stocks, Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argenaria SA (BBVA) (Spain), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSMC)(TSM) (Taiwan), and KLA Corp.(USA) (KLAC) were the strongest relative performers BBVA, a leading banking franchise across Spain and Latin America, saw shares rally amid robust economic growth in Spain, supporting lending growth (alongside falling interest rates). The company also initiated medium-term targets through 2028, which were well received by the market.

TSMC, the world’s leading semiconductor manufacturer, rose amid robust demand for chips due to rising computing intensity of models with growing usage and reasoning capabilities. KLA is a supplier of semiconductor equipment, including inspection and metrology, critical for manufacturing semiconductors. The stock outperformed strongly in 3Q, driven by robust demand for semi equipment, in particular for advanced logic, high band memory (HBM) and advanced packaging, fueled by the AI infrastructure investments.

The greatest individual detractors were the Fund’s positions in Carrier Global Corp. (USA)(CARR) and SAP SA (Germany) (SAP), and an underweight to Tesla (USA) (TSLA).

Carrier Global is a worldwide leader in energy solutions, including heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC). Shares declined as the industry struggled to set proper guidance, resulting from a regulated refrigerant change in the residential HVAC market. We remain positive on the name given the company’s current transformation into a pure play HVAC and refrigeration company, which we believe should be an above-trend market over the long term. SAP, the German enterprise software maker, declined amid broader software sector weakness despite strong underlying business growth. The outlook remains positive, in our view, with a continued product upgrade cycle augmented by growing AI opportunities. Tesla, the multinational electric vehicle company, outperformed amid hype around its Robotaxi and Optimus potential. (Optimus is a general-purpose robotic humanoid under development by Tesla.)

Outlook

We maintain a cautiously optimistic stance on equities, with a focus on relative value opportunities. While a resilient earnings outlook in the US supports the broader market, US equity index valuations remain stretched compared with historical norms, creating a high hurdle for earnings growth and increased scrutiny of future catalysts. Despite recent gains, we remain constructive on non-US developed markets, where valuations remain more attractive and macroeconomic conditions are improving.

In fixed income, tight spreads leave limited room for credit outperformance. We continue to favor US sovereigns and investment grade bonds over lower-rated segments, which remain more vulnerable amid economic uncertainty. Overall, we see quality fixed income as a useful hedge in portfolios given lingering macro risks and potential for further policy shifts. Additionally, we continue to believe that real assets have the potential to serve as a hedge against rising equity volatility and lingering inflation uncertainty.

In April 2025, Macquarie Group Limited and Nomura Holding America Inc. (Nomura) announced that they had entered into an agreement for Nomura to acquire Macquarie Asset Management’s US and European public investments business. The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, including the receipt of applicable regulatory, client, and shareholder approvals. Subject to such approvals and the satisfaction of these conditions, the transaction is expected to close by the end of 2025.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objectives, risk factors, charges, and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the Fund’s prospectus and its summary prospectus, which may be obtained by visiting macquarie.com/mam/literature or calling 800 523-1918. Investors should read the prospectus and the summary prospectus carefully before investing.

The views expressed represent the investment team’s assessment as of the date indicated, and should not be considered a recommendation to buy, hold, or sell any security, and should not be relied on as research or investment advice.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

Market risk is the risk that all or a majority of the securities in a certain market – like the stock market or bond market – will decline in value because of factors such as adverse political or economic conditions, future expectations, investor confidence, or heavy institutional selling. • A fund or strategy may not receive payment of principal, interest, and other amounts due in connection with investments in loans and other direct indebtedness. Furthermore, loans and other direct indebtedness may be less liquid than other investments and may not be considered securities that are subject to the protections of securities laws. • The value of commodities investments will generally be affected by overall market movements and factors specific to a particular industry or commodity, which may include weather, embargoes, tariffs, and economic health, political, international regulatory and other developments. Exposure to the commodities markets may subject the fund to greater volatility than investments in traditional securities. • Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk is the risk that an issuer of a fixed income security may be unable to make interest payments and/or repay principal in a timely manner. Interest rate risk is the risk that prices of bonds and other fixed income securities will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. Fixed income securities with longer maturities or duration generally are more sensitive to interest rate changes. • Investments in derivatives may involve additional expenses and are subject to significant loss if they perform differently than anticipated. Derivatives are also subject to the risk that the counterparty may fail to perform its obligations. • A fund or strategy that invests in an exchange- traded fund (ETF) will typically be exposed to the risks of the instruments in which the ETF invests and will bear its proportionate share of the ETF's fees and expenses. • International investing (particularly in emerging markets) may be adversely affected by political instability; changes in currency exchange rates; inefficient markets and higher transaction costs; foreign economic conditions; the imposition of economic or trade sanctions; or inadequate or different regulatory and accounting standards. Investing in emerging markets involves greater risk than investing in more developed foreign markets because, among other things, emerging markets may have less stable political and economic environments, information about issuers may be less available or of a lesser quality, and securities markets may be smaller, less liquid, and subject to greater price volatility. • High yield securities (“junk bonds”) are subject to reduced creditworthiness of issuers, increased risk of default, and a more limited and less liquid secondary market. High yield securities may also be subject to greater price volatility and risk of loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. • The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy’s investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them. • Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS) are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk and may also be subject to prepayment risk and extension risk. In addition, MBS and ABS may decline in value, become more volatile, face difficulties in valuation, or experience reduced liquidity due to changes in interest rates or general economic conditions. Certain MBS, such as collateralized mortgage obligations, real estate mortgage investment conduits, and stripped MBS may be more susceptible to these risks than other MBS. • Certain US government securities, such as securities issued by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac and the Federal Home Loan Bank system, are not backed by the "faith and credit" of the US government.

Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees, transaction costs, or expenses. Indices are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. The MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) represents large- and mid-cap stocks across developed and emerging markets worldwide. The index covers approximately 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set. Index “net” return approximates the minimum possible dividend reinvestment, after deduction of withholding tax at the highest possible rate. Index “gross” return approximates the maximum possible dividend reinvestment.

Institutional Class shares, Class R shares, Class R6 shares, and Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. See the prospectus for more information. Nothing presented should be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell any security or follow any investment technique or strategy.

All third-party marks cited are the property of their respective owners.

The Fund’s investment manager, Delaware Management Company (Manager), may seek investment advice and recommendations relating to fixed income securities from its affiliates: Macquarie Investment Management Europe Limited (MIMEL) and Macquarie Investment Management Global Limited (MIMGL). The Manager may also permit MIMGL to execute Fund equity security trades on behalf of the Manager. The Manager may also permit MIMEL and MIMGL to exercise investment discretion and perform trading for fixed income securities in certain markets where the Manager believes it will be beneficial to utilize MIMEL’s or MIMGL’s specialized market knowledge, and the Manager may also seek quantitative support from MIMGL. MIMGL is also responsible for managing real estate investment trust securities and other equity asset classes to which the portfolio managers may allocate assets from time to time.

On December 31, 2024, Delaware Funds by Macquarie was renamed Macquarie Funds.

Macquarie Asset Management (MAM) is the asset management division of Macquarie Group. MAM is an integrated asset manager across public and private markets offering a diverse range of capabilities, including real assets, real estate, credit, equities and multi-asset solutions. Macquarie Group refers to Macquarie Group Limited and its subsidiaries and affiliates worldwide. Investment advisory services are provided to the Macquarie Funds by Delaware Management Company, a series of Macquarie Investment Management Business Trust (MIMBT), a Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) registered investment adviser. The Macquarie Funds are distributed by Delaware Distributors, L.P., a registered broker/dealer and member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) and an affiliate of MIMBT.

Other than Macquarie Bank Limited ABN 46 008 583 542 (“Macquarie Bank”), any Macquarie Group entity noted in this document is not an authorized deposit-taking institution for the purposes of the Banking Act 1959 (Commonwealth of Australia). The obligations of these other Macquarie Group entities do not represent deposits or other liabilities of Macquarie Bank. Macquarie Bank does not guarantee or otherwise provide assurance in respect of the obligations of these other Macquarie Group entities. In addition, if this document relates to an investment, (a) the investor is subject to investment risk including possible delays in repayment and loss of income and principal invested and (b) none of Macquarie Bank or any other Macquarie Group entity guarantees any particular rate of return on or the performance of the investment, nor do they guarantee repayment of capital in respect of the investment.

data.title: SailPoint: Sailing To Calmer Waters
data.description:
When SailPoint, Inc. (NASDAQ:SAIL) went public in February of this year, I concluded that I was comfortable to let this identity security play sail by. Its private equity owner Thomas Bravo decided to bring the pioneer of SaaS business models public, relatively soon after it was acquired, back in 2022.

That felt a bit soon to me, and while the company was not saddled with debt (at least not pro forma the IPO proceeds) the offering felt a bit rushed. In between demanding sales multiples and largely realistic break-even levels achieved, the company has seen growth continue at a healthy clip this year, with some realistic profits appearing here.

Amidst a stagnant share price, appeal has been on the increase, yet that does not mean that appeal is really seen here in my book.

An Identity Enterprise Security Play

SailPoint is looking to manage and secure dynamic access to both critical applications and data within organizations. The idea behind its solutions, is to provide a comprehensive identity security, by unifying identity data across systems and machines.

This is key, as IT staff are battling with an ever more complex environment, in which employees have to access multiple sources of data from various applications, both at home and at work, as well as through different devices. With access becoming much more complex, the risks of compromised systems is only on the increase, with managing such IT environments becomes every more important.

Founded in 2005, the company went public at in 2017 and was acquired by Thomas Bravo in a $6.9 billion deal in 2022, as the rise in AI and cyberattacks provided a real backdrop for the company to go public.

IPO Discussions

SailPoint went public at $23 per share in February, a level at which the 546 million shares were valued at $12.6 billion, with the enterprise valuation being roughly the same amidst a flattish net debt load.

These are demanding valuations if we look at the cold hard numbers. For the fiscal year ending in January 2022, revenues were reported at $450 million, with GAAP operating losses reported at $60 million. Revenues rose some 23% to $552 million in 2023, yet GAAP operating losses ballooned to $300 million, largely driven by amortization charges from the acquisition of the business. Revenues rose another 27% to $700 million for the year ending in 2024, with operating losses increasing further to $332 million.

For the year 2025, the company looked to be on track to post revenues around $876 million, marking solid growth, as operating losses for the first nine months of 2024 came in at $158 million, down over a hundred million on the year before, as finally some progress was made on that front. At the prevailing valuation, the company was valued at 14 times sales and if we add back amortization charges, the company was basically breaking even.

That valuation was too demanding and while AI might provide a driver in demand for its solutions, it could also invite fresh competition for the likes of SailPoint, all of which made me very cautious at the time of the offering. This was the case, even as some of its peers traded at demanding sales multiples as well, although often companied by real profits.

Trading Flat

After having fallen to the mid-teens in the spring, shares have seen a recovery to current levels of $22 per share, trading flat compared to the IPO day, lagging the market at large a bit.

In the spring, the company reported a 23% increase in full year sales to $862 million, as GAAP operating losses narrowed to $188 million, after fourth quarter losses were reported at $30 million. These numbers are impacted by a combined $222 million combined amortization charge, revealing modest profitability if we adjust for this.

The company guided for a 19-20% increase in full year sales to a midpoint of $1.03 billion, with adjusted income from operations seen moving up to $151-$156 million. This number compares to a $133 million number in 2024.

Following two solid quarterly reports in the first half of the year, the company hiked the full year guidance in two occasions, now seeing full year sales up to a midpoint of $1.055 billion, with adjusted income from operations seen up to a midpoint of $179 million.

Note that first quarter earnings were impacted by elevated stock-based compensation expenses in connection to the IPO. A GAAP net loss of $10 million was reported in the second quarter, yet if we add back pre-tax amortization charges of a some $46 million, realistic earnings of around $36 million (albeit for taxes) emerged.

With 555 million shares trading at $22 per share, the market value of the firm stands at $12.2 billion. This includes a modest net cash position of $271 million now, for a $12 billion enterprise valuation. In comparison to the full year sales outlook, sales multiples have come down to 11 times, while the company has begun to report some real profits, although these are just cents on the dollar here.

What Now?

Compared to the situation in February, I am turning a bit more upbeat. In between a flattish valuation, the company has seen growth rates around 30% so far this year, while having demonstrated on operating leverage. At this point, realistic profits are now achieved, although minimal in relation to the prevailing valuation.

Resulting sales multiples have come down a lot, as growth, at least for now, is aided by the rise of AI agents and machine entities, but over time, AI inspired cybersecurity solutions could provide a more competitive threat as well.

As a result, I see appeal increasing a bit, but most of this is relative appeal. While real profitability is achieved here, these earnings are so minimal that no expected backing is seen from a valuation point of view anytime soon, at least not based on realistic earnings metrics.

data.title: First National Bank Alaska: Time To Engage The Final Frontier
data.description:
The financial sector has many different classifications when it comes specifically to banking institutions. There is a massive size variety in banking institutions. The Federal Reserve must stress test banks to ensure they are sufficiently risk-tolerant and able to survive even the worst of economic situations. During the Great Financial Crisis, a number of financial institutions that appeared strong on the outside collapsed because of their extreme risk-taking in the name of capital gains.

U.S. banking regulations contain four different categories of financial institutions. These would be from the largest of financial institutions that are considered to be globally systemically important banks, all the way down to financial institutions that have over $100 billion worth of total assets. The Federal Reserve has its own labeling system:

  • Global Systemically Important Banks
  • Large Banking Corporations
  • Regional Banking Organizations
  • Community Banking Organization

Recently, I covered an attractive preferred security that would fall into the category of a regional banking organization, because the financial institution Bank OZK (OZK) has over $10 billion of assets but less than $100 billion.

Today, I want to take some time and explore an opportunity that falls strictly into the community banking organization category. This would be a financial institution that is extremely focused on a locality, having less than $10 billion in assets, often meaning that they only have a handful of branches. These types of opportunities can also provide excellent opportunities for income or returns. These smaller banking organizations are usually laser-focused on shareholder returns and on providing exceptional quality service to their customers. If you have to compete against major banks that have billions of dollars worth of assets at their disposal at any given moment, as a small financial institution, you must provide an exceptional experience and have wonderfully strong underwriting to be able to compete effectively.

Let's dive in!

The Final Frontier

Alaska is commonly nicknamed the Last Frontier, largely because it is still heavily unpopulated and exceptionally wild. Alaska may seem like an unusual place to find a wonderful income opportunity, but institutions there are as rugged and as virtuous as the people who live there. You may think of Alaska mainly as grizzly bears and oil. But it's important to remember that among all the activity that goes on there, a financial institution of high quality must be present to provide the liquidity that's needed.

This is when we will turn our attention to Alaska's largest community bank - First National Bank Alaska (OTCQX:FBAK). FBAK was founded in 1922, and recently celebrated 100 years of continual operation. In this time, it has grown from one single location to 28 locations within Alaska.

When looking at a community-sized bank, I want to see that the financial institution not only has strong growth over its lifespan but is also recognized for strong customer service. Like I mentioned, if you are only operating on less than $10 billion of assets, you must have a stellar customer-focused experience; otherwise, you'll die out relatively quickly or be bought out by a larger firm. The fact that FBAK has been able to exist for 100 years and has not been taken over by a larger institution wanting to make a move into Alaska tells me that there is a reason for this.

FBAK has succeeded in winning multiple recognitions:

In 2025, Alaska Business readers voted First National “Best of Alaska Business” in the Best Place to Work category for the 10th year in a row, Best Bank/Credit Union for the fifth time, and Best Customer Service for the second year in a row. That year, Forbes also selected First National as the sixth best bank on their America’s Best Banks list and one of the top two Banks in the State, and Newsweek recognized the bank as one of the nation’s Best Regional Banks and Credit Unions. The bank was also voted “Best of Alaska” in 2024 in the Anchorage Daily News awards, ranking as one of the top three in the Bank/Financial category for the sixth year in a row. American Banker again recognized First National as a “Best Bank to Work For” in 2024, for the seventh consecutive year.

Recognizing that FBAK is well-liked by not only their employees but also their community, and their performance as a financial institution ranks it among the best, according to Forbes. It is easy to see why they have remained a standalone, independent financial institution. When Wells Fargo (WFC) decided to make an entrance into the Alaska market in a big way, it did so by buying out one of their peers.

Financial Performance

Now that we've taken a moment to explore some of the intangibles about why FBAK continues to exist and confirmed that it is a well-loved community bank, we must now take a look at its tangible financial performance.

Looking over the last four quarters of performance, we can see that the deposits within FBAK have dipped slightly. Meanwhile, their loan book has also grown slightly simultaneously. FBAK is still well capitalized; their loan-to-deposit ratio is still around the 71% mark. Historically, a ratio below 80% is considered a highly conservative approach.

This conservative nature is mirrored in their non-performing loan history. Historically, it has been in the 0.10-0.20% range. FBAK saw a spike this quarter to 0.38%. They maintain an allowance of 0.77%, meaning this spike is not a problem now, but should be watched going forward. To put this into perspective, Regions Financial (RF) sees a range of 0.70-0.80% in its non-performing loans reported. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) reported a range of 0.60-0.70%.

This once again highlights and underscores that FBAK is extremely conservative, not only in their originations and underwriting, but also in the level of capital that they leave exposed to lending. Lending will be the primary driver of returns for a financial institution of this size, and FBAK can carefully mitigate potential damage by being extremely selective in the loans that it originates.

This careful approach has allowed FBAK to steadily grow its earnings per share without exposing shareholders to extensive risk.

The Dividend

When I'm looking for an investment that will reward me for my ownership, I want it to primarily do so through the income it provides me via dividends and distributions. Financial institutions are historically recognized for having growing dividends but also low yields.

Often, institutions that have higher yields are riskier, largely because of the risk they've taken on or because of negative market sentiment that has driven down the share price, raising the yield. However, when you move out of the major financial institution, starting to climb your way down the ladder below the $100 billion mark, you can find higher yields simply because they are unrecognized or less well-known. With assets only in the $4 billion range, FBAK falls squarely into the relatively unknown category, and its yield of 7% is squarely covered by earnings.

Last quarter, FBAK reported an EPS of $5.80 while declaring a $4 per share dividend, 68% of earnings paid to their shareholders.

Over the years, FBAK has steadily grown its dividend, along with paying frequent year-end special dividends:

FBAK, like many financial institutions, is clearly a reliable, qualified dividend income-paying company.

Risks To Consider

Because FBAK is a community bank with exposure directly to the Alaskan state economy, the biggest risk that comes from this is some sort of economic calamity occurring specifically in Alaska. Many financial institutions of larger sizes can balance out the economic struggles of one area with the successes of another due to their exposure to both. But FBAK is solely focused on Alaska itself, and the health of the Alaskan economy is of essential importance.

At this point, the Alaskan economy continues to be strong. Overall, GDP growth in Alaska is slower than the 10-year average, but personal income growth is higher. This means that while the economy itself is not producing more growth than it has historically, individuals have more free income that they're generating to enjoy.

The other major issue to consider when trying to buy shares of FBAK is its extremely low volume. This can be readily mitigated by a healthy dose of patience and a judicious usage of a limit order.

Looking Forward

FBAK is expected to announce their earnings at the end of October or the very beginning of November, historically between Oct. 29 and Nov. 5. When reviewing the next report, I will be looking at their loan-to-deposit ratio and non-performing loans. Interest rate cuts historically spur more lending activity and reduce deposits - lower rates encourage spending. FBAK has historically kept a very conservative ratio and has room to grow their loan book effectively, even if deposits fall slightly.

A major concern would be if non-performing loans climbed higher. They have a strong cushion for their nonperforming allowance, but my preference is to see this percentage fall, not climb again.

Conclusion

FBAK is a well-run, highly conservative community bank offering 7% yields to new shareholders at current prices. It would find a ready home in one's taxable brokerage account due to the QDI that it pays out quarterly.

Investors looking to initiate a position in FBAK are strongly encouraged to use a limit order along with a massive dose of patience. It can take time to fill your order successfully, and if you want a very specific price or price range, being careful in setting that limit order can reward you over the long run.

When it comes to retirement, investing in the financial success of various sectors of the United States economy can provide you with wonderful returns over the long run. But careful examination of each opportunity is essential to ensure that it aligns with your desired goals and outcomes. There are millions of dollars traveling through the market daily and coming to the doors of investors just like you in the form of dividends and distribution checks. If you were not receiving those regularly, but you're struggling to make ends meet, today might be the time to adjust your portfolio.
